tate of Califorrzia 
Fair Political Practices CommissioIl 
P.o. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance/ Administration 
(916) 322-5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
March 8, 1977 
• Samuel V. McGrath 
City Attorney, Ricamond 
Richmond, California 
94804 
Enforcement/Conflict of Interest 
322-6441 
Re: 
Economic Development Commission of the City of Richmond 
Dear Mr. McGrath: 
This is in response to your letter to Chairman Lowenstein 
dated January 13, 1977. 
The Commission will not issue a formal 
opinion on this matter because it does not present a substantial 
question of interpretation of the Political Reform Act. 
See 2 
Cal. Adm. Code § 18320 (g) (2). 
However, I trust the following 
informal advice will enable you to resolve the matter. 
Taking your questions together, the basic issue presented 
is whether or not the members of the Richmond Economic Develop-
ment Commission (hereinafter the "EDC") are "public officials II 
within the meaning of Government Code §87100* and/or whether 
the Commission is an "agency" subject to the requirement that 
it adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government 
Code §87300, et seq. 
Section 82048 defines "public official" as "every member, 
officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government 
agency". 
The Commission has further defined "member" in §87000 
by regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700(1), which provides: 
(1) 
"Member" shall include, but not be limited 
to, salaried or unsalaried members of boards or com-
missions with decision-making authority. 
A board or 
commission possesses decision-making authority when-
ever: 
*All statutory references are to the Government Code 
unless otherwise indicated. 


Samuel V. McGrath 
March S, 1977 
Page Two 
(A) 
decision; It may make 
a final governmental 
(B) 
It may compel a governmental deci-
sion~or it may prevent a governmental decision 
either by reason of an exclusive power to 
initiate the decision or by reason of a veto 
which may not be overridden; or 
(C) 
It makes substantive recommendations 
which are, and over an extended period of time 
have been, regularly approved without signifi-
cant amendment or modification by another pub-
lic official or governmental agency. 
The powers and duties of the Economic Development Commission 
are defined in §3.14.090 of the Municipal Code of the City of 
Richmond, which states: 
3.14.090. 
Powers and duties. 
The specific 
powers and duties of the commission are as follows: 
(A) 
Assist city officials, develop, compile 
and coordinate information regarding available 
areas suitable for economic development in the 
city of Richmond by reviewing staff work and 
providing appropriate feedback; 
(b) 
Upon request of the city council, review 
proposed agreements for the lease, sale or use of 
city-owned land and properties for economic develop-
ment in the city of Richmond by revie\ving staff work 
and providing appropriate feedback; 
(c) 
Work with the port director on matters 
related to the economic development of the port 
of Richmond; 
(d) 
Review appropriate zoning ordinances and 
make recommendations to the planning director to 
encourage proper zoning and orderly development of 
areas suitable for economic developmen'ti 


Samuel V. McGrath 
March 8, 1977 
Page Three 
(e) 
Work with state and local agencies, 
governing bodies, public and private organiza-
tions as appropriate to encourage economic 
development of the city. 
(f) 
Promote the interests of commercial 
and industrial development in metropolitan 
Richmond; 
(g) 
Perform other tasks related to the 
city's economic development as requested by 
the city manager or the city council. 
Based on these duties and powers, we cannot conclude 
that the EDC is "decision-making" by virtue of possessing 
any of the authority referred to in subsections (1) (A) or 
(1) 
(B) of the cited regulation. 
There remains the question 
whether they are the type of board or commission referred 
to in subsection (1) (C)* 
We conclude that as the EDC is 
currently operating they are not. 
We are particularly 
persuaded of this because it appears the EDC has not, in 
fact, exercised those powers referred to in subsections 
(b) and (d) of the cited ordinance. 
Furthermore, we have 
reviewed minutes of the meetings of the EDC for a two-
year period. 
The tenor of those meetings can best be 
described as an exchange of information and views between 
certain city officials and the Commission members. 
Indeed, 
the minutes tend to reflect frequent "briefing" of the 
Commission by city officials and an information-gathering 
type of questioning of these officials by members of the 
EDC. 
Examples of this are the following meetings: 
July 14, 1976 - presentation by the Public Works 
Director of the City and a representative of Stan-
dard Oil concerning oil spills and cleanup. 
No votes taken. 
August 13, 1975 - presentation of the City Manager 
concerning redevelopment events. 
Commission voted 
to recommend establishment of an Economic Devel-
opment Department. 
*In determining whether a board or commission is "decision-
making" within the meaning of subsection (I) 
(C) of the 
regulation, we will analyze not just the apparent statu-
tory authority of the agency, but rather the practical 
effects and consequences of its decisions or deliberations. 


Samuel V. McGrath

OFFICE OF THE CITY ATTORNEY 
CITY OF RICHMOND 
CALIFORNIA 94804 
SAMUEL V. McGRATH 
(415) 232-1212, EXTENSION 351 
CITY ATTORNEY 
January 13, 1977 
The Honorable Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices COIllmission 
1100 11K" Street 
Sacramento, California 95814 
Dear Chairman Lowenstein: 
Please find enclosed herewith a copy of Chapter 3.14 of the 
Municipal Code oJ the City of Richmond, California. 
This Chapter 
created the Economic Development Commission of this City in 1974 
for the purpose of advising the !'City Council and city officials 
on proposed activities for development of the city's port and for 
other overall economic development of the city. If 
(Sect"ion 3 .14.020 
of Code.) 
The particular powers and duties of the Commission are 
set forth in Section 3.14.090 of that Chapter. 
This is to request a written opinion on the following questions: 
1. 
Is this City Commission a local government agency which 
must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code 
Section 87300? 
2. 
If the answer to the above question is in the affirmative, 
is each member of such Commission an individual whose position must 
be designated in such Code in view of the definition of "designated 
employee" in Government Code Section 82019(c) and in view of the 
fact that the City considers the Commission to be one which serves 
a solely advisory function? 
3. 
Is each member of such Commission a "public official!! 
within the meaning of Government Code Section 87100 who must comply 
with the requirements of that section? 
Would the answers to the above questions be the same if 
Sections 3.14.020 and 3.14.090 of Chapter 3.14 were amended to read 
as shown in the draft which I have attached to the enclosed copy of 
such Chapter? 


The Honorable Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
January 13, 1977 
Page 2 
I shall be happy to provide you with such additional information 
as you may require in order to grant th~s request for a written 
opinion. 
SVMcG:ss 
Enclosure 
Sincerely, 
Samuel V. McGrath 
City Attorney

3.14.060-3.14.090 
BOARDS AND COMMISSIONS 
such resignation, the vacancy shall be filled by appointment for the 
unexpired portion of term of the appointee's predecessor in the manner 
prescribed in Section 3.14.030 hereof. 
(Amended by Ordinance No. 56-74 N.S.) 
3.14.060 
Officers. The commission shall elect from among their own 
number a chairman and vice-chainnan who shall serve in such capacity for 
one year. In the case of a vacancy in the position of chainnan or 
vice-chainnan, the commission shall elect a successor who shall serve for the 
balance of the predecessor's tenn. The commission shall appoint a secretary 
who may be. but need not be, a member of said commission. 
3.14.070 
Meetings. The commission shall hold its first meeting within 
thirty days after all of its members have been appointed. At such meeting, 
the commission shall fix the time and place of regular meetings of the 
commission which shall not be less frequent than once each month. All 
meetings of the commission shall be open to the public. 
3.14.080 
Rules and records. The commission shall adopt rules for the 
transaction of the business of the commission, which rules shall, among 
ot~~ things, include the tenn of the secretary of the commission, the 
manner of calling and giving notices of special meetings and hearings, and the 
appointment and powers of standing committees. The commission shall keep 
records which shall be open to the public of its resolutions, transactions, 
motions, orders, findings and determinations. 
3.14.090 
Powers and duties. The specific powers and duties of the 
commission are as follows: 
(a) Assist city oft1cials, develop, compile and coordinate information 
regarding available areas suitable for economic development in the city of 
Richmond by reviewing staff work and providing appropriate feedback; 
(b) Upon request of the city council, review proposed agreements for 
the lease, sale or use of city-owned land and properties for economic 
development and make recommendations to the city council: 
(c) Work with the port director on matters relating to the economic 
developmen t of the port of Richmond; 
(d) Review appropriate zoning ordinances and make recommendations 
to the planning director to encourage proper zoning and orderly 
development of areas suitable for economic development; 
(e) Work with state and local agencies, governing bodies, public and 
private organizations as appropriate to encourage economic development of 
the city; 
(f) Promote the interests of commercial and industrial development in 
metropolitan Richmond; 
(g) Perfonn other tasks related to the city's economic development as 
requested by the city manager or the city counciL 
(Source: Ordinance No. 25-74 N.S.) 
(Richmond 10-15-75)

DRAFT 
SVMcG:ss 
1/11-77 
3.14.020 
Commission purpose. 
The purpose of this commission 
is solely to advise the city council and certain city officials 
concerning the matters set forth in Section 3.14.090 pertainins to 
the development of the city's port and the overall economic develop-
ment of this City. 
The Economic Development and Renewal Administrator 
or a representative nominated by him, shall attend all regular and 
special meetings of the commission. 
3.14.090 
Advisory duties. 
(a) 
The commission shall advise the City Council, 
City Manager, and Economic Development and Renewal Administrator 
concerning the following matters: 
(1) 
Available areas suitable for economic development 
in the City of Richmond; 
(2) 
Proposed agreements for the lease, sale or use 
of City-owned land and properties for economic development; 
(3) 
The economic development of the Port of Richmond; 
(4) 
Proper zoning and orderly development of areas 
suitable for economic development; and 
(5) 
Promotion of the 
interests of commercial and 
industrial development in metropolitan Richmond. 
(b) 
The acts of the commission shall be solely advisory 
and the commission shall not, therefore, have any authority to: 
(i) 
Make any final governmental decision or compel 
any governmental decision pertaining to any of the above matters or 
to any other governmental matter; or 
(ii) 
Prevent a governmental decision either by reason 
of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a 
veto which may not be overridden. 


(c) 
Neither the City Council nor any of the above-named 
officials shall be obligated whatsoever to approve any recommendation 
which the commission may make in carrying out its duties. 
Such 
Council and officials shall have full authority, in relation to 
any commission recommendation, to disregard the recommendation or to 
make significant amendments or modifications thereof.

Dear Chairman Lowenstein: 
Please find enclosed herewith a copy of Chapter 3.14 of the 
Municipal Code oJ the City of Richmond, California. 
This Chapter 
created the Economic Development Commission of this City in 1974 
for the purpose of advising the !'City Council and city officials 
on proposed activities for development of the city's port and for 
other overall economic development of the city. If 
(Sect"ion 3 .14.020 
of Code.) 
The particular powers and duties of the Commission are 
set forth in Section 3.14.090 of that Chapter. 
This is to request a written opinion on the following questions: 
1. 
Is this City Commission a local government agency which 
must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code 
Section 87300? 
2. 
If the answer to the above question is in the affirmative, 
is each member of such Commission an individual whose position must 
be designated in such Code in view of the definition of "designated 
employee" in Government Code Section 82019(c) and in view of the 
fact that the City considers the Commission to be one which serves 
a solely advisory function? 
3. 
Is each member of such Commission a "public official!! 
within the meaning of Government Code Section 87100 who must comply 
with the requirements of that section? 
Would the answers to the above questions be the same if 
Sections 3.14.020 and 3.14.090 of Chapter 3.14 were amended to read 
as shown in the draft which I have attached to the enclosed copy of 
such Chapter? 


The Honorable Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
January 13, 1977  
I shall be happy to provide you with such additional information 
as you may require in order to grant th~s request for a written 
opinion.

sections.conclusion: We conclude that as the EDC is currently operating they are not. We are particularly persuaded of this because it appears the EDC has not, in fact, exercised those powers referred to in subsections (b) and (d) of the cited ordinance. Furthermore, we have reviewed minutes of the meetings of the EDC for a two-year period. The tenor of those meetings can best be described as an exchange of information and views between certain city officials and the Commission members.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether members of the Richmond Economic Development Commission are "public officials" under Government Code §87100 and whether the Commission must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code under §87300, considering that it functions in an advisory capacity.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that EDC members are not "public officials" requiring conflict of interest compliance because the Commission lacks decision-making authority; it functions primarily as an advisory and information-gathering body without final governmental decision-making power, veto authority, or regularly approved substantive recommendations.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: OCR document with some text quality issues on final pages. Document includes both the incoming request (January 13, 1977 letter from City Attorney) and the FPPC response (March 8, 1977). FPPC explicitly states it will not issue formal opinion under 2 Cal. Adm. Code § 18320(g)(2) but provides informal advice. Conclusion section reconstructed from body text as there is no dedicated conclusion section header.

created the Economic Development Commission of this City in 1974 
for the purpose of advising the !'City Council and city officials 
on proposed activities for development of the city's port and for 
other overall economic development of the city. If 
(Sect"ion 3 .14.020 
of Code.) 
The particular powers and duties of the Commission are 
set forth in Section 3.14.090 of that Chapter. 
This is to request a written opinion on the following questions: 
1. 
Is this City Commission a local government agency which 
must adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code 
Section 87300? 
2. 
If the answer to the above question is in the affirmative, 
is each member of such Commission an individual whose position must 
be designated in such Code in view of the definition of "designated 
employee" in Government Code Section 82019(c) and in view of the 
fact that the City considers the Commission to be one which serves 
a solely advisory function? 
3. 
Is each member of such Commission a "public official!! 
within the meaning of Government Code Section 87100 who must comply 
with the requirements of that section? 
Would the answers to the above questions be the same if 
Sections 3.14.020 and 3.14.090 of Chapter 3.14 were amended to read 
as shown in the draft which I have attached to the enclosed copy of 
such Chapter? 


The Honorable Daniel H. Lowenstein, Chairman 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
January 13, 1977  
I shall be happy to provide you with such additional information 
as you may require in order to grant th~s request for a written 
opinion. 
SVMcG:ss 
Enclosure

CONCLUSION: We conclude that as the EDC is currently operating they are not. We are particularly persuaded of this because it appears the EDC has not, in fact, exercised those powers referred to in subsections (b) and (d) of the cited ordinance. Furthermore, we have reviewed minutes of the meetings of the EDC for a two-year period. The tenor of those meetings can best be described as an exchange of information and views between certain city officials and the Commission members.

BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 Technical Assistance/ Administration (916) 322-5660 Executive/legal 322·5901 March 8, 1977 • Samuel V. McGrath City Attorney, Ricamond Richmond, California 94804 Enforcement/Conflict of Interest 322-6441 Re: Economic Development Commission of the City of Richmond Dear Mr. McGrath: This is in response to your letter to Chairman Lowenstein dated January 13, 1977. The Commission will not issue a formal opinion on this matter because it does not present a substantial question of interpretation of the Political Reform Act. See 2 Cal. Adm. Code § 18320 (g) (2). However, I trust the following informal advice will enable you to resolve the matter. Taking your questions together, the basic issue presented is whether or not the members of the Richmond Economic Develop- ment Commission (hereinafter the "EDC") are "public officials II within the meaning of Government Code §87100* and/or whether the Commission is an "agency" subject to the requirement that it adopt a Conflict of Interest Code pursuant to Government Code §87300, et seq. Section 82048 defines "public official" as "every member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government agency". The Commission has further defined "member" in §87000 by regulation, 2 Cal. Adm. Code §18700(1), which provides: (1) "Member" shall include, but not be limited to, salaried or unsalaried members of boards or com- missions with decision-making authority. A board or commission possesses decision-making authority when- ever: *All statutory references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Samuel V. McGrath March S, 1977 Page Two (A) decision; It may make a final governmental (B) It may compel a governmental deci- sion~or it may prevent a governmental decision either by reason of an exclusive power to initiate the decision or by reason of a veto which may not be overridden; or (C) It makes substantive recommendations which are, and over an extended period of time have been, regularly approved without signifi- cant amendment or modification by another pub- lic official or governmental agency. The powers and duties of the Economic Development Commission are defined in §3.14.090 of the Municipal Code of the City of Richmond, which states: 3.14.090. Powers and duties. The specific powers and duties of the commission are as follows: (A) Assist city officials, develop, compile and coordinate information regarding available areas suitable for economic development in the city of Richmond by reviewing staff work and providing appropriate feedback; (b) Upon request of the city council, review proposed agreements for the lease, sale or use of city-owned land and properties for economic develop- ment in the city of Richmond by revie\ving staff work and providing appropriate feedback; (c) Work with the port director on matters related to the economic development of the port of Richmond; (d) Review appropriate zoning ordinances and make recommendations to the planning director to encourage proper zoning and orderly development of areas suitable for economic developmen'ti Samuel V. McGrath March 8, 1977 Page Three (e)
embedding.summary: The FPPC declines to issue a formal opinion but provides informal advice that members of the Richmond Economic Development Commission are not "public officials" under the Political Reform Act because the Commission lacks decision-making authority and functions primarily in an advisory capacity. The determination is based on analysis of the Commission's actual powers and the practical tenor of its operations over a two-year review period.
